- COVERAGE OF THE KEY MOMENTS — every event the core loop fires that a player should HEAR has a binding (or a deliberate NONE with a stated reason): the core verb, each reward, each penalty, the win, the lose. A thin layer that sounds only ONE moment (just the win) FAILS.
- COUPLING HONEST — every binding records `couplingToGameplay: {needs, surfaceExposed, kind:"event"}`, and every `surfaceExposed:false` also appears in the end-of-note GAP REPORT; missing/empty coupling records = 0.
- GENERALITY — no binding hard-codes a value only THIS game has where the event vocabulary + registry keys would do (the SOUND PATH prose may name the game's feel; the bindings are archetype-general).
**Red flags:**
- Generated / placeholder sound: minting a sound or referencing a `play` key the registry does not have — there is NO generator here.
- Invented / dangling event: an `on` no effect/custom capability emits, recorded as if resolved (`surfaceExposed:true`) — a sound that never plays. (Record `surfaceExposed:false` + log the gap; never silently dangle, never invent.)
- Grab-bag (no SOUND PATH): off-family picks with no stated sonic identity — sounds incoherent.
- Mismatched pick: a win that plays `oops-soft`, an error that plays `ta-da` — fights the moment (pick by the registry's role/tags).
- Thin layer: sounding only one moment and leaving the verb, rewards, and penalties silent.
- Guidance/effects bleed (Sound ⊥ Guidance ⊥ effects): authoring a coaching tip, or editing an effect, as part of the sound section — you bind a sound to an event and nothing else.
- A gameplay number authored, or reading a gameplay internal / a sibling fragment / the GDD to author sound — producer-scope/separation violation; the right move is HALT (`SOUND_NEEDS_VOCABULARY`) when the event vocabulary is missing, never reach inside.

## Model retrieval (model-director)
**Artifact:** model-queries.json — `{queries:[{slot,query,style,license,tier}]}` (the 3D sibling of asset-prompts.json; one library-retrieval search query per `type:'model'` slot. The driver's `gen/fetch_models.py` `run` post-hook consumes it → `public/assets/models/<slot>.glb` + `model-manifest.json`; W2's 2nd `reconcile` flips each model slot's `index.json` status; the runtime `core-3d` `ModelLoader3D` loads the GLB or falls through to the procedural primitive).
**Purpose:** A parallel producer (the 5th lane, sibling of the Asset art-director) that, for a 3D archetype, authors the retrieval queries that fetch a real GLB mesh per `type:'model'` slot — words, not meshes. It runs off the frozen blueprint and is INERT on a 2D run (zero model slots → `{queries:[]}`). It embodies the INVERTED 3D failure policy: a model is an ENHANCEMENT over the engine's procedural primitive, so a miss NEVER halts. Producer, not verifier: it authors the queries only — never fetches, never writes a GLB, never touches index.json or the 2D asset-prompts.json.
**Acceptance criteria:**
- ONE QUERY PER MODEL SLOT — exactly one `queries[]` entry per `blueprint.assetList[]` `type:'model'` slot, in blueprint order, slot ids VERBATIM; queriesAuthored == the model-slot count. A skipped or duplicated model slot FAILS.
- CONCRETE RETRIEVAL KEYWORDS — each `query` is short, matchable low-poly-library nouns drawn from the slot's `description` + `searchHints` (e.g. "low-poly sedan car", "blocky humanoid character"); a vague one-word label or a full sentence FAILS.
- LICENSE + TIER — every entry sets `license` (default `cc0`, the cleanest for an automated pipeline) and `tier:"library"` (never `"generate"` — not implemented).
- NO-OP ON 2D / NO MODEL SLOTS — when the blueprint declares zero `type:'model'` slots, the artifact is exactly `{"queries":[]}` with status `empty` (the normal 2D case — NOT a failure).
- NEVER HALTS ON A MODEL CONCERN (inverted policy) — a model slot with a thin description still gets a best-effort query (from the slot id + searchHints); the node does not HALT and does not block (only `model-queries.json` is required; a retrieval miss degrades to the procedural primitive downstream).
- IN LANE — authors `model-queries.json` (+ MEMORY.model.md) only; never writes a GLB, public/assets/models/**, index.json, asset-prompts.json, or any src/**/spec/** file.
**Red flags:**
- A query for a NON-model (2D sprite/tileset/audio) slot — the lane queries only `type:'model'` slots.
- `tier:"generate"` or a missing license — the only implemented tier is `library`; cc0-first.
- A vague label ("car", "guy") that a library can't match — the query must be concrete retrieval keywords.
- HALTing on a 2D run or a thin-description model slot — the inverted policy says never halt; write `{queries:[]}` (2D) or a best-effort query (thin slot).
- Fetching/downloading a model or writing a GLB / model-manifest.json itself — that is the driver's `run` post-hook, not the node.
- Querying every block/the ground/the bulk city — models are for the hero objects (player + a few landmark/interactable kinds); ground + the InstancedMesh city stay procedural (design-rules §7).

---

# The VOXEL / OPEN-ENDED SANDBOX case (per-node, 2026-06-15)

_These are the JUDGING criteria for the `voxel_sandbox` archetype + the `meta.goalModel: 'open-ended'` (creative-sandbox) branch — what a GOOD open-ended-sandbox artifact looks like at each producing node. They are the standard the human eye and the DEFERRED open-ended verify pass judge against; they are NOT instructions injected into a node (the producing nodes are archetype-agnostic and carry no voxel prose — the voxel craft lives in `templates/modules/voxel_sandbox/design-rules.md`). Each node's WIN-LOSE entry above still holds for every 2D win-lose game; this block is the additive open-ended bar. Ground truth: `design-rules.md` (the CHECKs), `world-data.ts` (the `VoxelWorldData` shape), `blueprint.schema.json` (`goalModel`/`voxelWorld`/`referenceInteraction`)._

## W1 Spec — open-ended sandbox GDD (w1-spec, goalModel:open-ended)
**Artifact:** spec/gdd.md (prose body + milestones tail) for a creative voxel sandbox
**Purpose:** Harden formalizes this into the open-ended blueprint; the build instantiates a first-person voxel world. Because an open-ended sandbox has no win to prove, the design's whole burden is a LEGIBLE, building-INVITING world and HONEST no-win framing — the world the player leaves behind IS the artifact, so a thin/empty or quest-faked design poisons the only readout there is.
**Acceptance criteria (what good looks like):**
- The GDD sets `meta.goalModel: 'open-ended'` (a creative sandbox, no win/lose loop) and frames it HONESTLY: the readout is the WORLD the player builds + the materials held (`worldBlockCount` / `inventory`), NOT a score; `meta.objective` reads as an INVITATION ("Mine and place blocks to build the world"), never a quest verb ("reach the exit", "defeat the boss").
- A LEGIBLE component set: a block palette of 3–6 DISTINCT role-named types (ground / wall / accent / glass — distinct hue OR role, never two interchangeable greys), and (if the prompt themes the space) a few themed interactive objects — small delta, consistent interaction.
- A building-INVITING starting world is designed as ground → open volume → seed: a continuous floor the player rests on, a majority-AIR volume above it (room to build INTO), and at least ONE seed structure (a low wall / a pillar pair / a small platform) that silently teaches "blocks stack into things" — never an empty void / flat grey plane.
- The world is BOUNDED + modest (a ~16³–48³ grid band) with `renderDistance` matched — sized to the draw-call + no-clip budget, not "satisfyingly huge".
- Milestones assert VERB-FUNCTIONAL OBSERVABLE state over `window.__GAME__` (mining removes a block → `worldBlockCount` decrements + `inventory` gains; placing adds one back; the player moves + is grounded + cannot clip a solid) — Given/When/Then over real observables (`player.position`/`worldBlockCount`/`inventory`/`targetBlock`/`selectedBlock`), never an implementation call, never a forced terminal `won`/`lost`.
**Red flags:**
- A win / lose / SCORE forced onto a sandbox: a points counter "to chase", a fake terminal `won`/`lost`, a quest-framed objective, or a `loseCondition` — the bolted-on-score / fake-win defect `design-rules.md` §4 exists to prevent (status staying `'playing'` forever is LEGAL here, not a bug).
- An EMPTY VOID start: a flat fill / grey plane with no seed structure, nothing to mine or build on near the spawn (the blank-page freeze).
- A muddy palette (1–2 types, or two same-colored types), or a sprawling oversized world that blows the budget.
- VOXEL SPECIFICS leaking into the GDD that should come from `design-rules.md` / the registry (re-listing engine capabilities, hard-coding `reach`/`miningSpeed` craft the design-rules own, or naming Minecraft / a theme noun in a behavior contract rather than in `meta.artStyle` + the block/object DATA).

## Harden — open-ended voxelWorld blueprint (harden, goalModel:open-ended)
**Artifact:** spec/blueprint.json with `meta.goalModel:'open-ended'` + `layout.voxelWorld` (no win-lose proof)
**Purpose:** The frozen design W2 materializes into `src/world/world1.json`. For an open-ended sandbox the schema RELAXES the win-lose contract (`winCondition`/`loseCondition`/`feasibility`/`referenceSolution` omitted) and instead requires a complete `voxelWorld` + a verb-functional `referenceInteraction` — so Harden's burden shifts from "prove winnable" to "prove the world is consistent + the verbs are functional".
**Acceptance criteria (what good looks like):**
- A COMPLETE `layout.voxelWorld` matching the `VoxelWorldData` shape (`world-data.ts`): `worldSize` (integer grid), `voxelSize`, `playerSpawn` (CONTINUOUS world units), `blockPalette` (3–6 entries, each `{id≥1,name,color|assetSlot,solid,transparent?}`), and a `startingLayout` with BOTH a ground (`fill` plane) AND ≥1 `structure`/seed (the §0 CHECK) — every coordinate authored, none left for the executor to invent.
- `declaredRanges` covers every block/spawn placement coordinate it perturbs, and `meta.goalModel:'open-ended'` is set so the schema `allOf` correctly relaxes the win-lose required-set (a win-lose proof MUST be absent here, not present-but-vestigial).
- A `referenceInteraction` verb SCRIPT (the open-ended sibling of referenceSolution): an ordered sequence of the documented verbs (mine / place / move / interact) each driving a NAMED interim `__GAME__` observable transition (mine → `worldBlockCount`−1 + `inventory`+1; place → back; move → `player.position` changes; no clip through a solid) — proof-by-existence that the core verbs are functional and the world responds.
- WORLD-CONSISTENCY proven on the numbers: every `startingLayout` cell + `playerSpawn` is IN-BOUNDS of `worldSize`; every `block` id RESOLVES in `blockPalette`; the `playerSpawn` sits in a NON-SOLID cell RESTING on the ground (not embedded in a solid, not floating over a void); the player is never boxed into an inescapable 1-cell pocket (the no-clip envelope `design-rules.md` §6).
- `meta.artStyle` carries the theme (subject-free global style); the palette colors + any `interactiveObjects[].assetSlot` carry the look — no theme noun hard-coded into a behavior contract.
**Red flags:**
- A WINNABLE proof on a sandbox: a `winCondition`/`loseCondition`, a `feasibility`/`referenceSolution` win-replay, or a score-bounded proof present on a `goalModel:'open-ended'` blueprint (it should carry `referenceInteraction` + world-consistency instead) — or `goalModel` left `win-lose`/absent on a clearly creative-sandbox prompt so the schema wrongly demands the win-lose set.
- An OUT-OF-BOUNDS layout: a structure/sparse-block/spawn cell outside `worldSize`, or a `block` id with no `blockPalette` entry — a phantom the materialization can't resolve.
- SPAWN EMBEDDED IN A SOLID (or floating over a void): the continuous `playerSpawn` lands inside a solid voxel or with no ground beneath — a no-clip soft-lock / fall at world start (the 3D analogue of the platformer spawn-off-platform defect).
- An empty-void `startingLayout` (a `fill` plane only, no seed structure), a muddy/oversized palette, or a sprawling world past the budget band — the design-rules CHECKs failed.

## W2 Scaffold — voxel materialization (w2-scaffold, archetype:voxel_sandbox)
**Artifact:** src/world/world1.json (the materialized VoxelWorldData) + STRUCTURE.md + index.json, atop the core-3d+voxel scaffold
**Purpose:** `VoxelWorldScene` reads `src/world/world1.json` to instantiate the WHOLE world from data; if it drifts from `blueprint.layout.voxelWorld`, or the wrong base/config-group is used, the build renders a different world than the one frozen.
**Acceptance criteria (what good looks like):**
- `src/world/world1.json` is a FAITHFUL VERBATIM projection of `blueprint.layout.voxelWorld` into the `VoxelWorldData` shape (`world-data.ts`): `worldSize`/`voxelSize`/`playerSpawn`/`backgroundColor` + `blockPalette[]` + `startingLayout` (`fill`/`structures`/`blocks`) — every value carried from the blueprint, no coordinate/palette-id/structure invented; strict-JSON-valid; it OVERWRITES the committed generic default `world1.json` (the per-game world ships, not the sample).
- The engine base is correct: `templates/core-3d` copied (read `coreBase:'core-3d'` off the genre record) THEN `templates/modules/voxel_sandbox` overlaid so the module wins — the project is the 3D shell, not the 2D `core`.
- The hardened config merged under the right group: every tunable under `voxelConfig` (read `configGroup:'voxelConfig'` off the genre record — `moveSpeed`/`gravity`/`jumpSpeed`/`reach`/`miningSpeed`/`renderDistance`), NOT `playerConfig`; `screenSize`/`debugConfig`/`renderConfig` infra groups untouched.
- STRUCTURE.md names `VoxelWorldScene` as the data-driven scene that instantiates the world from `src/world/world1.json`, and documents the 3D `window.__GAME__` hook (player.position / worldBlockCount / inventory / targetBlock) per the voxel hook contract.
**Red flags:**
- The committed DEFAULT `world1.json` shipped UNCHANGED (the generic sample world) instead of the per-game world materialized from the blueprint — the build renders the default sandbox, not the designed one.
- WRONG CONFIG GROUP (voxel tunables merged into `playerConfig`/dropped) or the wrong base copied (`core` instead of `core-3d`) so the 3D module has no engine to overlay.
- A voxelWorld value paraphrased / rounded / invented in `world1.json` (a palette color, a structure coord, the spawn), the file omitted/non-JSON, or world GEOMETRY written into a `.ts` instead of this data file.

## W4 Execute — voxel delta (w4-execute, archetype:voxel_sandbox)
**Artifact:** src/** = ONLY the interactive-object `custom[]` delta (or NOTHING for a basic creative sandbox), atop a green build
**Purpose:** The voxel SDK already instantiates the world + runs mining/placement/inventory/collision from data — so W4's job is ONLY the genuinely-novel interactive-object delta (the themed `$custom:<id>` object behaviors), if any. For a basic creative sandbox `custom[]` is empty and W4 writes nothing but (at most) a shell.
**Acceptance criteria (what good looks like):**
- W4 authors ONLY the `blueprint.custom[]` interactive-object delta (each a module-SDK-interface file resolved via the voxel `custom-registry` seam) — and EMPTY `custom[]` ⇒ no per-game code at all (the standard mine/place/inventory/no-clip verbs are SDK-built from the world data + the registry, NEVER re-implemented by W4).
- NO world coordinate / block placement / palette / spawn written into a `.ts` (that is data in `src/world/world1.json`, W2's job); the engine (store/mesher/collider/FPS controller/raycaster) is untouched.
- The build is genuinely green (`tsc --noEmit && vite build` exit 0) with no engine file stubbed/deleted; on a missing blueprint number W4 HALTs (records the gap, writes nothing), never invents.
**Red flags:**
- RE-IMPLEMENTING THE ENGINE: writing a voxel store / mesher / collider / mining / placement / FPS controller in a `.ts` — re-paying the ~780-line engine cost the module exists to absorb (the reference-competitor anti-pattern).
- PLACEMENT CODE: writing block/structure/spawn coordinates or the palette into a `.ts` instead of leaving them in the materialized world data; or a non-empty `custom[]` for a basic sandbox that needs none.

## VERIFY-2 QA — DEFERRED open-ended verify pass (verify-2-qa, goalModel:open-ended)
**Artifact:** the open-ended verify report (the verb-functional probe set) — _DEFERRED: this verify MODE is not yet built (companion mode skips the verify nodes; see the map's M6b deferred carry-forwards)._
**Purpose:** The open-ended analogue of the win-lose QA gate. A sandbox has no win to replay, so the gate replays the `referenceInteraction` verb-script and asserts the verbs are FUNCTIONAL + the world is CONSISTENT — impl-fidelity, NOT gameness. This is the STANDARD a future open-ended verify pass (handoff §6) must meet; until built, the human eye is the gate.
**Acceptance criteria (what good looks like, when built):**
- The VERB-FUNCTIONAL probe set runs on the real built game and each probe carries observed before/after: MINING-REMOVES-BLOCK (`worldBlockCount` decrements + the targeted cell becomes air + `inventory` gains one of that type), PLACEMENT-PERSISTS (a placed block stays + `inventory` spends one, an illegal place is a clean no-op), and INVENTORY-CONSERVES (the metamorphic oracle: mine then place back → `inventory` net-zero, world net-unchanged).
- NO-CLIP holds (the swept-AABB collider output: the player cannot pass through a solid voxel) and the player stays IN-BOUNDS of `worldSize` — the substrate guarantees re-checked on the real build.
- The verdict is impl-FIDELITY (the verbs realize the frozen `referenceInteraction` over real `window.__GAME__` state), NEVER a gameness/fun judgement and NEVER a forced terminal `won`/`lost` (status staying `'playing'` is correct + expected for an open-ended game).
**Red flags:**
- Re-judging GAMENESS / "is it a fun sandbox" instead of impl-fidelity; or expecting/forcing a terminal `won`/`lost` and flagging its absence as a failure (it is legal for open-ended).
- A probe that injects the observed outcome (setState the `worldBlockCount`/`inventory` under test) instead of driving the real mine/place verb from a placed precondition; or a no-clip/bounds gate skipped on the 3D build.

## action_3d archetype — the 2nd 3D substrate (WIN-LOSE, archetype:action_3d)
_The per-node JUDGING criteria for the `action_3d` archetype (the third-person/high-angle Three.js character-action module on `coreBase: core-3d`). UNLIKE the voxel block above, action_3d is WIN-LOSE — every WIN-LOSE node entry above (W0/W1/Harden/W2/W4/VERIFY-2) STILL HOLDS in full (winCondition/loseCondition/feasibility/referenceSolution/coupling/declaredRanges are all required and judged exactly as for a 2D game). This block adds ONLY the archetype-specific materialization bar — the `layout.actionWorld` → `world1.json` projection isomorphic to voxel's `world1.json` projection. They are a fixture for the human eye + the DEFERRED verify pass, NOT prose injected into a node (the producing nodes are archetype-agnostic; the action_3d craft lives in `templates/modules/action_3d/design-rules.md`). Ground truth: `action-world-data.ts` (the `ActionWorldData` shape), `blueprint.schema.json` (`$defs/actionWorldLayout`), the genre record's `projections`._

### Harden — action_3d actionWorld blueprint (harden, archetype:action_3d, goalModel:win-lose)
**Artifact:** spec/blueprint.json with `meta.archetype:'action_3d'` + `layout.actionWorld` + the full win-lose proof
**Purpose:** The frozen design W2 materializes VERBATIM into `src/world/world1.json`. action_3d keeps the FULL win-lose contract; the ONLY archetype difference from a 2D game is the `layout.actionWorld` variant (not the 2D playerSpawn/goal/bounds triple).
**Acceptance criteria (what good looks like, beyond the win-lose Harden bar above):**
- A COMPLETE `layout.actionWorld` matching the `ActionWorldData` shape (`action-world-data.ts`): `seed`/`blocks`/`size`/`spawn` (CONTINUOUS world units, on the walkable ground) + `streets[]` (≥1, each `{id,axis:'x'|'z',at∈[0,1]}`) + `keyLocations[]` (each `{id,position,interactable,kind,zoneId}` — `kind` a DATA render hint, never a game-name literal) + a `walkableGraph` (`zones[]` + `adjacency`) + the `objectives[]` mission chain (each `{id,text,targetAnchor?,completeOnInteract?}` — the win seam is `completeOnInteract` naming an interactable key-location, NOT a hardcoded id comparison) + the optional `systems[]` scene-system bindings — every value authored, none left for the executor to invent.
- `feasibility.archetype:'action_3d'` and the reachability proof iterates `walkableGraph.zones` over `adjacency`: the objective target anchors (`near:<id>`/`region:<id>`) all resolve to a keyLocation/zone, and `spawn` sits in a walkable zone (the 3D analogue of spawn-on-ground).
**Red flags:**
- A `targetAnchor`/`completeOnInteract` that names no keyLocation/zone (a dangling anchor the worldCue + win check can't resolve); a `spawn` outside every walkable zone; or a game NOUN baked into `kind`/`text` as if it were a schema constant rather than design DATA.

### W2 Scaffold — action_3d materialization (w2-scaffold, archetype:action_3d)
**Artifact:** src/world/world1.json (the materialized ActionWorldData) + STRUCTURE.md + index.json, atop the core-3d+action_3d scaffold
**Purpose:** `ActionScene` reads `src/world/world1.json` to instantiate the WHOLE city from data (generation, spawn, key-locations, interactables, mission objectives); if it drifts from `blueprint.layout.actionWorld`, or the wrong base/config-group is used, the build renders a different world than the one frozen.
**Acceptance criteria (what good looks like):**
- `src/world/world1.json` is a FAITHFUL VERBATIM projection (the driver's 1:1 `copy` of `blueprint.layout.actionWorld`) into the `ActionWorldData` shape — every field carried (`seed`/`blocks`/`size`/`backgroundColor`/`spawn`/`streets`/`keyLocations`/`walkableGraph`/`objectives`/`systems`), none invented or rounded; strict-JSON-valid; it OVERWRITES the committed generic default `world1.json` (the per-game city ships, not the sample).
- The engine base is correct: `templates/core-3d` copied (read `coreBase:'core-3d'` off the genre record) THEN `templates/modules/action_3d` overlaid so the module wins — the project is the 3D shell, not the 2D `core`.
- The hardened config merged under the right group: every tunable under `actionConfig` (read `configGroup:'actionConfig'` off the genre record — `moveSpeed`/`gravity`/`jumpSpeed`/`turnSpeed`/`fireRate`/`interactionRange`/`renderDistance`), NOT `playerConfig`; `screenSize`/`debugConfig`/`renderConfig` infra groups untouched; `gameConfig.shell`/`guidance`/`controlsHelp`/`objective`/`failModel` carried verbatim per the gameConfig projection (HUD at `gameConfig.shell.hud`, coaching at `gameConfig.guidance.coaching`).
- STRUCTURE.md names `ActionScene` as the data-driven scene that instantiates the city from `src/world/world1.json`, and documents the 3D `window.__GAME__` hook (player.position/facing/health/isGrounded · ammo/selectedWeapon · currentObjective/objectivesCompleted/nearestInteractable) per the action_3d observable palette.
**Red flags:**
- The committed DEFAULT `world1.json` shipped UNCHANGED (the generic sample city) instead of the per-game world; WRONG CONFIG GROUP (action tunables in `playerConfig`/dropped) or the wrong base copied (`core` instead of `core-3d`); or an actionWorld value paraphrased/invented in `world1.json` (a spawn coord, a keyLocation, an objective anchor), the file omitted/non-JSON, or world GEOMETRY written into a `.ts` instead of this data file.

### W4 Execute — action_3d delta (w4-execute, archetype:action_3d)
**Artifact:** src/** = ONLY the `custom[]` delta (themed `$custom:<id>` behaviors + any `systems[]`-bound scene system) atop a green build
**Purpose:** The action_3d SDK already instantiates the city + runs the controller/camera/weapon/interaction/mission verbs from data + the bound capabilities — so W4's job is ONLY the genuinely-novel `custom[]` delta (resolved via the module's `custom-registry` seam), if any. The win-lose W4 bar above holds in full.
**Acceptance criteria (what good looks like):**
- W4 authors ONLY the `blueprint.custom[]` delta (each a module-SDK-interface file — `IBehavior`/`ISceneSystem` — resolved via `custom-registry`); EMPTY `custom[]` ⇒ no per-game code at all (move/aim/shoot/switch/interact + the mission/interaction managers are SDK-built from the world data + the registry, NEVER re-implemented).
- NO world coordinate / keyLocation / spawn / objective written into a `.ts` (that is data in `src/world/world1.json`, W2's job); the engine (controller/camera/weapon/interaction/mission managers/minimap) is untouched; the build is genuinely green (`tsc --noEmit && vite build` exit 0); a missing blueprint number ⇒ HALT, never invent.
**Red flags:**
- RE-IMPLEMENTING THE ENGINE: writing the capsule controller / follow camera / hitscan weapon / interaction or mission manager in a `.ts`; PLACEMENT CODE: writing city/keyLocation/spawn/objective data into a `.ts` instead of the materialized world data; or a non-empty `custom[]` for a game whose verbs all resolve in the registry.

## Affordance bar — per player-facing COMPONENT (the `presents` demo bar)
_A per-COMPONENT JUDGING bar (NOT a per-node bar) for the affordance-demo quality loop. Every existing gate certifies STATE (a component exists, builds, its observable changes) but never the RENDERED AFFORDANCE (the held tool, the on-screen button, the muzzle flash / tracer) — so a component can ship invisible or wrong and only the human-on-play catches it. The loop has each player-facing component declare a `presents` block (visible affordance + inner triggers + a required demo) gated by `registry:check` incl. `check-demos` (the affordance twin of the registry harness's gallery `demoProps` gate). This bar is what the DIAGNOSIS + INDEPENDENT-JUDGE sub-agents (and the human eye reading the Studio still) judge a component's `presents`/demo against — it is **NEVER injected into a producing node's prompt** (teach-to-the-test); the loop lives in `add-archetype/SKILL.md` §3 + the module's `registry/check-demos.mjs` gate + the `presents` registry field, never in a node. Archetype-general (the worked instance is action_3d; the bar names no game noun)._
**Artifact:** a player-facing component's `presents` block (in `discover.mjs`/`capabilities.json`) + its `demos/<id>.demo.ts` + the Studio still
**Purpose:** A registered player-facing component (a held weapon, an interact prompt, a muzzle/tracer effect, an on-screen control) must RENDER its affordance and FUNCTION under its triggers — not merely exist and mutate state. `check-demos.mjs` fails `registry:check` if any player-facing component lacks a passing demo, so the human review surface structurally cannot lag the catalog. If the demo is missing or non-rendering, the component can ship invisible/wrong with every state gate still green.
**Acceptance criteria (what a good `presents`/demo looks like):**
- VISIBLE AFFORDANCE PRESENT — the demo renders the component's affordance on screen (the held tool / button / effect is actually drawn), not an empty mount; the Studio still shows it at a readable size for the human eye.
- EACH TRIGGER HAS A VISIBLE CONSEQUENCE — every inner trigger the `presents` block declares is FIRED in the demo and its consequence is OBSERVABLE (the button depresses, the muzzle flashes, the tracer travels, the prompt appears); a declared trigger with no rendered consequence = FAIL.
- THE DECLARED CONTROLS RENDER — the inputs/affordances the component claims to surface (a fire control, an interact key prompt) are actually drawn and reachable in the demo, matching what the player would see in-game.
- RESET-SAFE — the demo mounts/unmounts (or re-runs) cleanly with no residual state across triggers, so a second fire/interact behaves like the first (no latched effect, no frozen affordance after the first event).
- PROP-DRIVEN — the demo drives the component through its real props/bindings (the same `presents`-declared handles the game binds), never a hard-coded internal poke; the affordance reads correctly across the prop variants the component supports.
- NO GAME NOUN — the `presents` block and the demo are archetype-general: the affordance/trigger vocabulary names the verb and the surface, never a single game's noun baked in as if it were a constant (the component renders the SAME way for every future game that binds it).
**Red flags:**
- A component registered with NO `presents` block or NO passing demo (it slips the `check-demos` gate, or the gate was not run) — the invisible/wrong-affordance class this loop exists to catch.
- The demo mounts but renders nothing (an empty affordance), or a declared trigger fires with no visible consequence — a green build that ships a dead/invisible component.
- The demo pokes an internal to fake the consequence instead of driving the real prop/binding (the affordance "works" only in the demo's special-case), or it is not reset-safe (the second trigger behaves differently because state latched).
- A game noun baked into the `presents` block or the demo (an affordance hard-coded to one game's entity) instead of a prop-driven, archetype-general affordance.

## Exposed-event bar — per player-facing COMPONENT (the `surface().events` + `.emit()` bar)
_A per-COMPONENT JUDGING bar (NOT a per-node bar) for the event-protocol responsiveness loop — the OBSERVABLE twin of the Affordance bar above. Every existing gate certifies STATE and (with `check-demos`) the RENDERED AFFORDANCE, but neither certifies that a component's MOMENTS are OBSERVABLE to the guidance/shell/verify tier — so a component can ship its triggers as cosmetic `fireEffect` side-channels and every cue bound to them is silently DEAD (the `$custom:ChargerGoal`/`ContactRespawn` dogfood: cues bound to `charger.activated`/`player.respawn` never fire because the moment reaches neither the bus nor the log). The loop has each player-facing component DECLARE its moments in `surface().events` and EMIT them on the shared `EventBus`, gated by `check-wiring` (claim ≡ emit) + `check-exposes` (the `drivenBy → expect` responsiveness gate). This bar is what the DIAGNOSIS + INDEPENDENT-JUDGE sub-agents judge a component's `surface().events`/emit sites against — it is **NEVER injected into a producing node's prompt** (teach-to-the-test); the loop lives in `implement-milestone/SKILL.md` §3.1a + `add-archetype/SKILL.md` §3 + the module's `registry/check-wiring.mjs`/`check-exposes.mjs` gates + the `surface().events` declaration, never in a node. The canonical contract is §8 of design/event-protocol-design.md. Archetype-general (the bar names no game noun)._
**Artifact:** a player-facing component's `surface().events` declarations (the EventDecl `{name,payloadType,scope,drivenBy,expect}` claims) + its real `.emit()` sites on the shared bus + the generated event-catalog row
**Purpose:** A registered player-facing component's gameplay moments (a goal unlocked, a respawn, a block mined, a charger activated) must be OBSERVABLE to the shared consumer tier — declared in `surface().events` AND emitted on the bus so the `TriggerEngine` log-poll can fire an `on-event` cue and the verify harness can assert the transition. A moment fired only through cosmetic `fireEffect` reaches neither the log nor a paired observable, so a guidance/shell cue bound to it is dead and no gate catches it — exactly the workaround this protocol replaces.
**Acceptance criteria (what a good event surface looks like):**
- EVENT DECLARED — every player-facing moment the component publishes is declared in `surface().events` as `{name:'<domain>.<verb>', payloadType, scope, drivenBy, expect}`; an emit absent from `surface().events` is undeclared = invisible to the catalog/gate.
- REAL EMIT ON THE BUS — each declared name has a real `.emit()` site on the shared `EventBus` at the moment it happens (`check-wiring`: claim ≡ emit), never a private side-channel the log can't tap.
- LEAN SERIALIZABLE PAYLOAD — the payload is IDs/primitives only, JSON-serializable (mandatory for the log/replay); never a live class instance (it loses prototype semantics on serialize).
- DRIVENBY → EXPECT OBSERVABLE — the declared `drivenBy` verb produces the declared `expect` transition, observable in the event log AND/OR a paired observable (`check-exposes` boots the witness, fires `drivenBy`, asserts `expect` — the runtime stub-killer).
- NO COSMETIC-FIREEFFECT-ONLY — `fireEffect` is for juice only; a gameplay moment a cue/another system reacts to MUST emit on the bus (the cue binds to the emitted event), not ride a `fireEffect('<name>')` string that never reaches the log/observable.
- NO GAME NOUN — the event names + the surface are archetype-general: the `<domain>.<verb>` vocabulary names the moment, never a single game's noun baked in (the same event fires for every future game that composes the component).
**Red flags:**
- An event declared in `surface().events` with NO `.emit()` site (`check-wiring` red) — the declared-but-dead stub this protocol exists to kill.
- An emit on the bus for an event ABSENT from `surface().events` — undeclared, so invisible to the catalog/gate.
- A gameplay moment fired ONLY via `fireEffect('<name>')` (or any private side-channel) so a guidance/shell cue bound to it is silently dead — the `$custom:ChargerGoal`/`ContactRespawn` defect class.
- A `drivenBy` whose `expect` transition `check-exposes` cannot observe (the moment moves no log entry nor paired observable) — a runtime stub.
- A live class instance (or a non-serializable payload) shipped on the bus — breaks the log/replay.
- Editing the `exposes`/demo/oracle to pass a gate instead of fixing the emit (anti-reward-hack — a mis-declared event HALTs and escalates to the author), or a game noun baked into an event name.

_Maintained by the hermes-skill-system node-validation loop — sharpen a node's criteria whenever an edit changes what good output for that node means; edit by hand when a node should emit a richer shape._
